one of the UNAM kids — a girl named Luciana, project on rainwater capture for low-income housing in Iztapalapa, real one, scholarship student, lives with her grandmother and two cousins in Tláhuac, takes the metro 90 minutes each way to studio and back — made an unannounced toast back. she stood up with her glass and said 'arquitecta Camila, le quiero decir que cuando quería renunciar al programa, me dijo dos cosas: que mi proyecto importaba aún si nadie lo construía nunca, y que renunciar no era una traición a mis padres sino a mí misma. me quedé. estoy aquí. gracias.' Camila — who is composed, who is the most composed person I know in any room — wept. real tears, not movie tears, real ones, the kind that come from somewhere the person didn't know they had. she had to hand the wine glass to Patricia because her hand was shaking. I wept. I weep at engineering all-hands when someone announces they're leaving and I weep at architecture toasts apparently. Hugo wept with the unselfconsciousness of an old Brazilian man who has nothing left to prove. the visiting London partners were a little stunned, in the British way — one of them, a tall woman named Frances something, kept dabbing her eye and pretending it was the wind, although we were on a rooftop with no wind. it was a moment. a real one. those are rare in any context but particularly rare in corporate ones, where the architecture of the event itself usually prevents real moments. Patricia had built a small space — twenty minutes, no PowerPoint, an unscripted toast invitation — and the moment found its way in.

after the speeches there was a thing where the mentees each put a small pebble — the firm did this, it's a thing they apparently do for these — into a bowl on the table, and Patricia said 'each of these is a project we'll watch.' very Foster + Partners, slightly precious, but also kind of beautiful. each pebble was river-smooth, dark gray, sourced from a quarry in Hidalgo apparently (Patricia mentioned this; she would). Camila was given the bowl to keep. it's now on our kitchen counter. nube tried to eat one of the pebbles when we got home, we caught her in time, she got a stern talking to and then a treat to soften the blow.

the mentees came up to Camila one by one afterwards. Luciana cried again and Camila held her by the shoulders and said something I couldn't hear. another kid, named Esteban, told her about a competition he was entering and showed her sketches on his phone — Camila was already squinting at them and giving him feedback before her wine was finished. that's the kind of mentor she is. there's no off button. there's no 'this is my night' button. she is incapable of receiving an honor without immediately spending the moment of attention on someone else. it is not a strategy. it is who she is. it is one of the four or five things about her that I sometimes look at and think 'I do not know how to be that.' I am working on it. I will work on it for the next forty years.

late, on the cab ride home through the empty Reforma, the city blurry through the window, Camila — half asleep on my shoulder — said 'tengo miedo' and I said 'de qué' and she said 'de que la vida sea tan buena que se voltee' and I held her and we drove in silence the rest of the way through Reforma into Roma. that's the thing about the year we've had. we've learned that joy and grief don't take turns. they come at the same time. you have to carry both. and tonight she carried both. and so did I. and Hugo, in the back seat asleep with his head against the window like an old man on a long bus, was carrying three — joy for his daughter, grief for his wife, fatigue for the long Sunday ahead — and his face in sleep was the face of a man who had survived something terrible and arrived at something good. that's what 70 looks like. that's the threshold he is approaching. I want to make him a chess board worthy of it. I want our kitchen counter to keep its bowl of pebbles. I want our living room to fill, slowly, over the next decade, with these accumulated objects of attention paid: pebbles, vinyl records, cedar boards, a black-belt patch eventually. that's the apartment I want to grow old in. with this woman. and this dog. that's the project. that's the actual long-term project, the one all the others are subordinate to.

one thing I want to record because it might disappear if I don't. when Patricia finished her toast and Camila stood up to respond, Camila did not respond by talking about herself. she did not list her achievements. she did not thank the firm. she stood there for a moment, took a breath, looked at the UNAM students sitting at the front table, and said 'lo que tengan ustedes que decir esta noche, lo escucho. lo escucho yo y lo escucha mi mamá donde sea que esté.' she opened the floor. she gave the moment to them. then she sat down. that is when Luciana stood up. that is when the toast happened that made everyone cry. Camila engineered that. she did not say to herself 'I am the recipient of this honor and I should be at the center of this moment.' she said to herself 'the people I have been mentoring are at the center of this moment, and I should yield to them.' and she did. and the moment happened. and afterwards in the cab, when she said 'tengo miedo de que la vida sea tan buena que se voltee,' she meant: I am afraid of being a person who deserves this. she meant: I am afraid of believing I have earned the joy. she has earned the joy. she will not believe it for a while. that's also part of who she is. ándale. amen.

let me also note for the record what we did when we got home. nube had thrown up a small pile of trazodone-orange foam on the rug, which we cleaned up while still in our jumpsuit-and-linen-shirt outfits because nube does not care about your Pritzker-nominee status, nube cares about your ability to clean a rug at 11:30pm. Hugo took the guest room. Camila and I sat on the kitchen floor — again the kitchen floor, the locus of all our important moments — and ate cold leftover pasta directly from the tupperware with two forks. neither of us had eaten dinner. she leaned her head on my shoulder. nube curled at our feet. the bowl of pebbles sat on the counter above us. that was the end of the day. that was 11:47pm on a Friday in October 2026. I will remember the bowl of pebbles. I will remember the cold pasta. I will remember Camila's hair smelling of rooftop wind. I will remember nube's snoring. ándale. esto fue. esto pasó.

month 11 wrap. mom's lump benign. back PT working. memory leak fixed in rust. Camila's career hit a ceiling-shaking moment. pose-est paused gracefully. Camila's dad in town for a weekend. me lighter than I have been in months. I'm going to take a quiet weekend. listen to Beny Moré. read Chiang. drink mate. do dead bugs. that's the whole agenda.

if I look at this month from the outside it is one of the most consequential months of my life — both a near-disaster averted and a real piece of forward progress on almost every axis I care about. and yet what I want to do this weekend is read a Ted Chiang short story collection slowly with mate at hand and not think about any of it. that is also a sign of growth, I think. there was a younger version of me that would have used a weekend like this to plan the next thing — the MIT app, the chess board purchase trip to Toluca, the rust v0.15 design doc. and there is some part of me that still wants to plan all of those. but the wiser part of me — the part that did the dead bugs every morning for two weeks straight — knows that the right move after a high-amplitude month is rest, not more amplitude. so I am going to rest. I am going to drink mate. I am going to listen to records. I am going to take nube to the park twice a day. I am going to make Camila pancakes on Saturday morning the way she likes them, with banana inside the batter, with a little vanilla. that is the entire agenda. it is sufficient. it is more than sufficient. ándale.

let me also write down — because months tend to blur if you don't pin them — the specific shape of the things this month gave me. mom's CT scan came back benign, which is the dominant fact of the month, and which I have not yet fully metabolized. I think it will keep arriving in waves over the next year. some Tuesday in February I will be at my desk and I will think 'mom is alive' as a separate fact, freshly, again. back PT — week 4 of 6 — is unambiguously working; I can sit through a workday now without the right-side spasm I had been hiding for two months. memory leak in the rust v0.14 candidate is fixed; the actual root cause was a tokio JoinHandle leak in the retry layer, which I should have caught in v0.13 and which I am writing this down to remember to put into the post-mortem doc. Camila's Pritzker nomination — that one I will think about for the rest of my life. Hugo Sr.'s visit was the gentlest weekend of the year. pose-est paused gracefully — Itzel and Mariana have moved to NeRF stuff and we three are still going to grab dinner monthly even without the Discord, which matters more to me than I expected. the side project closing without rancor is the kind of thing I will quietly be proud of in five years.

I want to write one more thing about month 11 because it pertains to the kind of year I have had overall. this is the third month in a row where I have ended the month with a higher proportion of 'I rested' than 'I crammed.' that is a step-change in the way I do months. younger me would have closed month 11 with a sprint into month 12. current me is going to close month 11 by reading Chiang on Sunday afternoon with mate. that's the difference. ándale. me lo gané. nube too. she also rested. she is currently asleep on my left foot.

Roberto pulled me into a one-on-one this afternoon and pitched me on a transfer. Brújula opened a Bogotá office last quarter — they want a tech lead for the infra team there. someone who already knows our stack, has shipped a rust service, can stand up the team from scratch. they want me. eighteen months in Bogotá, full relocation, 25% salary bump, equity refresh, quarterly flights back to CDMX, the whole thing. they'd ship me and Camila and nube. January start. report directly to the CTO. I told Roberto I'd think about it. he said 'two weeks.' I went to the bathroom on the third floor afterwards and stood at the mirror and said 'no manches' out loud. I am writing this from the apartment. Camila is in São Paulo with her dad this weekend. nube is on my chest. apartment too quiet. have to call Camila. don't know what I want her to say. don't know what I want me to say.

let me also note the meta-context. Bogotá is, structurally, where Brújula is going to build out its Andean-region presence over the next 24 months — the Bogotá office is supposed to anchor Colombia, Peru, Ecuador for the logistics product. that's a real growth bet. tech lead there is not a sideways move. it is a step up. I would manage an infra team of 4-8, which I have never done — I have lead-engineered teams informally, but I have not had hiring authority or perf-review authority. that is the thing I would actually be learning over the 18 months. it is, on paper, the right next step for someone on a track to senior staff in five years. I am the right age for the offer. I am the right tenure for the offer. the offer is well-formed. and yet. ándale. una cosa a la vez. una cosa a la vez. mañana a Camila.

let me also note what Roberto specifically said, in his exact phrasing, because I don't trust myself to remember the wording in two days. he said 'Sebastián, te tengo una propuesta importante. el CTO me preguntó por nombres para Bogotá y dije el tuyo. es tu nombre el que está en la mesa.' he said 'la mesa' as in 'the table' — like the decision is happening at a table I'm not at. classic Roberto. he then said 'pero tú tienes la última palabra.' which is true, formally, and also slightly threatening in the way it was said — like, 'you have the last word, but the rest of the table has already used theirs.' I noted the framing without flinching. he then walked me through the package on his laptop. it was actually a solid package. he wasn't lowballing me. the equity refresh number was real — I haven't done the math but it's probably 15-20k USD over the four-year vest. I left the room and went to get water from the kitchenette and when I came back to my desk I had three Slack DMs from Camila about her flight to São Paulo and one from mom about a leftover-tamale recipe. life kept going. that's the thing about big offers. they happen on a Tuesday afternoon. and then your mom asks about tamales and you have to answer that too. ándale.

honest sub-thought: when Roberto said 'Bogotá' my first feeling was a flash of validation. ego is a real input. I noticed it before doing anything else with it. I have been a senior at Brújula for 2.5 years without a title move. tech lead would be the rung above. that is real. ego flash was about the rung. I am not going to take Bogotá to satisfy the rung. but I am writing it down so future me knows the rung was a real input I subordinated. ándale, jefe. te respondo en una semana.

told Camila tonight when she got back from São Paulo. she dropped her bag at the door, kissed nube, kissed me, sat on the couch. I told her the whole pitch — Bogotá, 18 months, 25% bump, full reloc, January start, report to CTO, headcount budget, the works. she put her book down before I'd even started the second sentence. she did not say anything for what felt like a minute after I finished. then she said 'sebas, qué te late.' which is 'what's your gut.' she didn't say her gut. she asked mine.

I told her my gut, in this order, slowly, because I was figuring it out as I said it:

1. it would be a real career step — first international leadership role, ground-up team building, exactly the kind of resume line that opens senior staff doors at any company in five years. that's not nothing. that's the kind of opportunity that doesn't come twice when you're a senior engineer in Mexico without a US passport.

2. 25% bump is not nothing. our rent went up 18% three months ago. savings would balloon. another 35-40k USD/year. that's not nothing either. that's a down payment in three years. that's MIT tuition possibly.

3. Roberto wants me out of his hair, I think a small portion of this is political. he probably wants someone less senior in the CDMX role for a few years, someone he can manage more directly. that part smells. but it's not necessarily a no — political offers can also be real opportunities, the political layer doesn't disqualify the substance underneath.

4. she just got Pritzker-nominated. her firm is here. her career is here. her dad flies in here. asking her to relocate now is asking her to put her career in storage for 18 months while mine accelerates. F+P has a Bogotá office but the Pritzker mentor program is here. Patricia is here. the UNAM kids are here. the bowl of pebbles on the kitchen counter is here.

5. nube would hate the move. she is anxious already. cargo flight, new climate, new sounds, new park system. it would set her back a year on the thunder thing.

6. mom is in Guadalajara. CDMX-Guadalajara is a 1.5h flight, 50 minutes if you're lucky. CDMX-Bogotá is 5+. if mom calls about another lump, I want to be 90 minutes away, not eight hours.

7. dad has angina. he's stable but he's 66. same logic.

8. Profe Esteban is irreplaceable. there are great gyms in Bogotá but my coach is in Colonia Juárez. and I have a black belt test in 6-8 months. I need him.

9. PT with Iván. mid-rehab. moving cities mid-rehab is a setback I do not want.

10. but. but. when do these offers come. it's hard to say no to the first international leadership offer of your life when you're 33 and ambitious and have just survived a year that included a manager change, a promotion, a near-miss with my mother's life, a side project closure, and a girlfriend's career-defining nomination. I have been telling myself for years that I'd take a leadership role abroad if it came. it has come. the form of it is wrong. the timing is wrong. but the offer is real.

she listened. she did not interrupt. when I was done she said something I am going to remember forever. she said 'puedo seguirte si lo decides. tengo opciones en Bogotá, F+P tiene oficina ahí, podría hacer una rotación. no quiero pero podría.' she did not flinch. she was offering to come with me. she meant it. and that — the fact that she meant it without hesitation, while also saying 'no quiero,' which she did not need to add but added because she is incapable of being dishonest in her own kitchen — was actually the moment I knew. because if she'd hesitated I'd have been able to lean on the hesitation. I'd have been able to say 'see, even she isn't sure, this isn't right.' her not hesitating put it back on me. it's mine to decide. she's not going to be the one who killed it. she's not going to martyr her career to give me an out. she is too generous for that and too honest for that. she would come. she would come without complaint. she would come and make it work and she would lose 18 months of momentum on the Pritzker year and she would do it and she would not blame me. but I would know what it cost her. and that would change us.

I told her I needed a few days. I'd build a spreadsheet. I'd talk to Daniela — my old manager Daniela Otero, the one who left for the Series A startup back in month 8, the one who knows me and knows Brújula's politics. I'd talk to Pablo. I'd talk to her dad — actually no, I won't talk to her dad about it, that's not fair to him. I'd talk to my mom. I'd talk to therapy if we could get a slot before the deadline. she said 'lo que necesites.' she meant that too. then she put her glasses back on, which she had taken off when I started talking, and went back to her book and nube curled tighter against her side and I went and made tea and stared out at the rooftops and at the planters Doña Marta tends on her balcony two floors up, full of geraniums she's had for thirty years, and I thought: this is the apartment. this is the city. this is the rooftop. this is the woman. this is the dog. this is the man two floors up's geraniums. these are the coordinates of my life right now and they are good coordinates. you don't trade good coordinates for a career line. or you do but you have to be honest about what you're trading.

I am 80% sure already. I am writing it here so future me can hold me to it. I am 80% sure I'm going to say no. but I'm going to do the spreadsheet and the call to Daniela first, because saying no without the diligence would be cowardly, and saying no after the diligence would be informed. I owe Roberto an informed answer. I owe me an informed answer. I owe Camila an informed answer. but my gut already knows. and my gut tonight is sitting on the couch with this woman and this dog and the geraniums two floors up are catching the late light and I am not leaving.

one more thing for the record. when Camila said 'no quiero pero podría,' it was the first time in five years I have heard her admit, openly, that she has a strong preference about her own life. she has always — always — submerged her preferences into mine. when we moved in together it was 'where do you want to live' and I said Roma Norte and she said 'me parece bien.' when we got nube it was 'do you want a dog' and I said yes and she said 'me parece bien.' when I started kickboxing she said 'me parece bien.' I have rarely heard her say 'I want X' as a primary preference. she is so accommodating it has been, over the years, a worry of mine — am I getting what I want at the expense of what she wants? and tonight she said 'no quiero.' two words. and they meant: I have a preference, sebastián. my preference is to stay in CDMX. I am not going to make this decision for you, but I am going to tell you my preference, and I am going to trust you with it. that is the first time. I am writing it here so that future me does not forget it. she said 'no quiero' and I heard it. and I think — I think — that's the end of the discussion, even though I will go through the spreadsheet motions for two more days for ego's sake. she said 'no quiero' and so we are not going. ándale, mi amor. respeté tu voz.

addendum, after Camila went to bed: I went out to the balcony with nube on a leash. she did her thing on the corner of the planter box (Camila will yell about this in the morning, but the planter is mostly dirt and a sad spider plant, nube has chosen it as her territory and we have lost that battle). I stood at the railing with my hands on the iron and looked across at the rooftops. there is a particular quality of light in Roma Norte at 11pm in October — the streetlights are old sodium-yellow, not the LED ones the city has been replacing them with elsewhere, so the light is warm and a little dim and it makes the cantera-stone facades look like something from a 1955 movie. you cannot get that light in Bogotá. you cannot get that light in Houston. you cannot get it in Buenos Aires or in Medellín. it is here. it is specific to this five-block radius. and I love it. and I love the fact that I have learned to love it. two and a half years ago I was a kid from Tijuana who had just moved to CDMX for a job and thought of this neighborhood as 'the one with the cafés.' now it is 'the one with the corner where Doña Marta's daughter sells flowers on Sundays' and 'the one with the tiles in the entryway of our building that Camila photographed for a project' and 'the one where nube has a friend, the gray cat from across the street, that she stalks every morning.' specificity is what love is. love does not happen at the level of the city. it happens at the level of the corner. it happens at the level of the tile. you cannot port love. you can only re-grow it elsewhere from a smaller starting point. I am not done growing this one. I do not want to start over.

I went back inside and Camila was already asleep, book open on her chest, glasses still on. I took the glasses off and put them on the nightstand and put the book on the floor. I lay down next to her. nube curled at the foot of the bed. and I lay there for ten or fifteen minutes thinking about the offer one more time and the answer was the same as it had been at 8pm on the couch. it is no. it has been no since the moment Roberto said the word 'Bogotá.' the next 48 hours are formality. ándale.

ok pasting the spreadsheet. I built it tonight in my notebook in pencil before I made it digital because I wanted the slow act of writing each line by hand. then I typed it up. here it is, full version. I am going to leave this here and stare at it for two days before I decide.

=== INTUITION ===

  - the engineer in me wants the CV line.
  - the woodworker in me wants the Toluca cedar.
  - the boyfriend in me wants Camila's year to be hers, not mine.

the scoreboard reads: stay. but the engineer says go. that tension is the actual decision. I'm going to sleep on it. seven nights. then I'll call Daniela. then I'll talk to Camila again. then I'll decide. but I am writing down here, in this private little log, that my gut said stay tonight, before I built the spreadsheet, and I want future me to remember the gut said stay first. the spreadsheet was an exercise in respecting the engineer in me. it was not an exercise in deciding. the deciding was the kitchen-counter geraniums-light moment. the spreadsheet just confirmed it.

I will say one thing for the record though. if Camila had said 'no, sebas, no quiero ir,' the decision would have been easy and trivial. she didn't say that. she said 'puedo seguirte.' and that means I have to be the one to choose. and choosing this — choosing to say no, choosing CDMX, choosing the apartment, choosing the chess board, choosing my mother being 90 minutes away, choosing Profe Esteban as my coach for the next decade, choosing to bet on the MIT app — choosing all of that is a decision, not a default. it is a decision I will own. I will not look back at age 50 and say 'I should have gone to Bogotá.' I will look back at age 50 and say 'I chose CDMX in 2026 and I lived the life that grew out of that choice and it was a good life.' that's the frame. that's what I'm choosing into.

there's a sub-frame I want to also write down. I am going to spend my career, if I do this right, making a series of decisions like this one. not to leave Mexico. not to chase the bigger title. not to trade good coordinates for a career line. some of those decisions will be wrong, in the sense that they cost me money or visibility or speed. but they will be the decisions of a person who knows where his ground is. and the ground is here. it's been here for two and a half years and it has only gotten more here. CDMX is mine now. Roma Norte is mine now. Atlas Box is mine now. Camila is mine and I am hers. nube is ours. mom and dad are accessible. PT is happening. the chess board is starting. the rust ship is happening. the MIT app is going. all of this is here. I am here. saying no to Bogotá is just saying yes to here.

or I'm 80% sure. let me sleep on it for two more days.

last addendum, 1am: I just looked up the historical climate for Bogotá. average daily high in January is 19°C. average daily low is 8°C. it rains 105 days a year. CDMX averages 22°C in January and 100mm of rain in the entire year. so Bogotá is colder and much wetter. nube hates cold rain. that doesn't disqualify it but it adds a little. ándale. tonight my decision is no. tomorrow we'll see. but it's no. it's no.

second addendum, 1:35am, cannot sleep: I keep coming back to one thing. the Pritzker nomination is the single most consequential moment of Camila's career so far, and it landed less than three weeks ago, and the next 12 months are going to shape what kind of architect she is for the next decade. asking her to put that in storage so that I can put a leadership-line on my CV is, structurally, asking her to subordinate the most important year of her career to mine. I cannot do that. I will not do that. even if she said 'puedo seguirte.' especially because she said 'puedo seguirte.' the only way to honor that sentence is to not make her cash it in. ándale. that's the answer. that's been the answer. I am going to sleep now. for real.

called Daniela. former manager Daniela, the one who left Brújula for the Series A startup back in month 8. she answered, glass of wine in hand, in her new home office. I told her about Bogotá. she laughed first. then she said 'Roberto is moving you, mijo.' I asked what she meant. she said 'they don't put their best rust engineer 5,000km away from the rust service in production unless someone wants the local credit.' she said it without rancor — Daniela doesn't do rancor. she said 'go if you want the line on your CV. don't go if you can't sleep with leaving Camila in the firm year. you decide.' she also said she'd write me an MIT rec letter if I asked, no questions. I almost cried. I told her thank you. I'll ask. soon.

Daniela also gave me a few practical things. she said 'if you do go, negotiate hard. double the relocation. ask for moving insurance specifically for the vinyl. ask for a guaranteed return role at CDMX in 18 months in writing, not as an aspirational phrase. ask for a housing stipend in the first 90 days, not just temp housing. ask for two return flights a quarter, not one. these are all available, they just need to be asked for. don't let them lowball you on the move because you're flattered by the offer.' that is the kind of advice nobody else in my life would give me. that is Daniela. she was my best manager and she is now my best ex-manager. I will never have a manager that good again. I told her so. she laughed and said 'you'll have one again, you'll just have to find them, sometimes you have to leave to find them.' that line stuck with me. she meant something specific by it. I am still chewing on what. ándale, Daniela. te debo una. más de una.

I also want to record what Daniela's voice sounded like during the call because the Daniela I worked with day-to-day was someone I knew physically — her presence in a room, the way she'd lean against a doorframe with a coffee mug — and the Daniela I now know via video call is a slightly different person, lighter, less guarded. she has lost about five kilos since leaving Brújula. her hair is longer. she is wearing a T-shirt with a startup logo I don't recognize. she said her new commute is 30 minutes of bicycle into Coyoacán and she has been swimming twice a week. she said 'the company is hard but it is hard in the way I want hard to be — the work is the hard thing, not the politics.' she said it casually but I heard it. she was telling me something about my own situation. she was saying 'the politics at Brújula are not the work, and that is data.' that is data. that is data I have been ignoring. I have been telling myself for six months that Roberto is just a manager, just a transition, just a phase. but maybe Roberto is the work now. maybe the work has become the politics for me. that is a depressing thought to write down at 11pm but it is, I think, true. and Bogotá would not be a fix for that. Bogotá would be a geographic distraction from a problem that is portable. so the answer is still no, but I'm noting that the question 'how long can I do this Brújula gig with Roberto as my manager' is now active. it has joined the queue. I will think about it next month. for now: Daniela said no with grace, and I will say no with grace too. ándale.

two days into thinking and I already know. I know I know. I'm going to say no. the spreadsheet was a ritual to give my ego a fair hearing. the gut won. the gut is right. Camila's year is here. mom's flight is from here. PT clinic is here. nube's anxiety, the vinyl shop, Doña Marta's bread, the Roma Norte rooftop, the bowl of pebbles, my mat at Atlas Box that Profe Esteban will hand me a black belt on someday. the answer is no. I'll tell Roberto next week. I'll wait two more days for the noise in my head to die down so I tell him cleanly, no, with a smile, with thank you for thinking of me, with respect, but with no. then I'll write to Daniela about the rec letter for MIT. one door closes. one door opens. ándale.

I want to also note for myself, here at the close of month 11, that this is the first big career offer I have actively turned down in my life. there was a smaller one back at Wizeline in 2020 — a transfer to the Houston office — that I declined without much agonizing because Camila and I had just started dating and the timing was obviously wrong. this one is different. this one is bigger, the timing is ambiguous, the stakes are real. and I am still saying no. and I am noticing that saying no, here, makes me feel more grounded rather than less. it is the opposite of the FOMO I expected. apparently when you turn down something that is wrong for you for clear reasons that you have written down, you feel more like yourself afterwards, not less. that is data I want to remember for the next time. the next time will come. probably also from Brújula, probably in 18 months when the Bogotá role has opened a regional pipeline and somebody else is leading it and they want a senior engineer to anchor a major component. I will face it then with a year and a half of additional context. I will probably say yes or no based on the same kinds of considerations I weighed tonight. ándale. December. me, Camila, nube, the apartment, the chess board sketch on the wall. that's the whole agenda. that is more than enough.

one final addendum that I want to record before this month ends and the noise settles. I am noticing that the act of writing down 'I am saying no' has, itself, made the no feel more permanent. there is something about the journal that converts ambivalence into commitment. I have known this abstractly for years. tonight I am watching it happen in real time. by the time I close this entry the no will be structural. I will not relitigate it. that is one of the small powers of writing. ándale. cerrado el mes. al doce.

one true sub-thought before I sleep. there is a version of this decision where I pick yes — Bogotá in January, lead an infra team, push my career forward two years in eighteen months — and that version of me is also valid. that version of Sebastián lives in a glass apartment in Chapinero, walks his dog through Parque El Virrey at 5pm, has an office on the eighth floor of a Brújula tower, hires four engineers in Q1 and four more in Q2, ships a regional version of RouteOpt by month 12, comes home for Christmas with a salary 25% higher and a wife who has either rotated to F+P Bogotá or has spent eighteen months on extended unpaid leave. that version of Sebastián is a real person. I can see him. I am, today, choosing not to be him. and I want to write that down as a respectful no, not a dismissive no. the version of me that goes to Bogotá deserves my respect. he is not a coward, not greedy, not imprudent. he is just the man who picked the other path. tonight I am picking this. Roberto gets his answer tomorrow. ándale.

real last note. I am sitting on the kitchen floor at 1:18am. Camila asleep. nube curled at my hip. pebble bowl on the counter. cedar planks against the wall. rust v0.14.3 green on the dashboard. mom alive. dad alive. Pablo five blocks away. Doña Marta two floors up. the answer is no. el corazón ya sabía. la cabeza tardó dos días. ándale.
